description:
You are assisting a database administrator who wants to do a quick health-check on a daily JSON dump that the monitoring system leaves in /home/user/db_metrics/.  

The directory already contains two files:  
1. /home/user/db_metrics/query_metrics.json – a JSON document with the day’s per-query execution statistics.  
2. /home/user/db_metrics/query_metrics_schema.json – a Draft-07 JSON-Schema that formally describes what a valid query-metrics file must look like.  

Your job consists of three small subtasks that must be carried out entirely from the Linux command line (no GUI editors).  Use only standard CLI tools that are normally present in a modern container image (jq, cat, echo, etc.).  Do NOT move, rename or modify the two source files mentioned above.

Subtask A – Schema validation  
• Validate that /home/user/db_metrics/query_metrics.json conforms to /home/user/db_metrics/query_metrics_schema.json.  
• Produce a one-line text file called /home/user/db_metrics/validation.log that contains exactly the word  
  VALID  
  if the document passes the schema check, or exactly the word  
  INVALID  
  otherwise.  Finish the line with the usual terminating newline (ASCII 0x0A).  

Subtask B – Find slow queries  
• With jq, filter all query objects whose execution_time_ms value is strictly greater than 2000 (i.e. > 2000, not ≥ 2000).  
• Write them to a new pretty-printed JSON file called /home/user/db_metrics/slow_queries.json in the following shape (key names and array name must match exactly, order inside the objects is not important but keep the id/statement/execution_time_ms triplet):  
  {
    "slow_queries": [
      { <query-object-1> },
      { <query-object-2> },
      ...
    ]
  }

Subtask C – Append an ID list  
• After the first VALID / INVALID line in /home/user/db_metrics/validation.log, append a second line that starts with the literal string  
  SLOW_IDS:  
  followed by a comma-separated list (no spaces) of the id fields of every query that ended up in slow_queries.json, in the same order they appear in that file.  A terminating newline is required as well.  Example pattern (do not use these concrete IDs — use what actually comes from the dump):  
  SLOW_IDS:Q124,Q231,Q400  

The final validation.log therefore always has exactly two lines.  If the schema check fails you still have to create slow_queries.json and the second line of the log (it may be empty after the colon if no slow queries are found).

Make sure:
• You do not create any additional files or directories other than the two mentioned output files.  
• The two output files have default user-writable permissions (umask-controlled, chmod 644 is fine).  
• Path-names and letter-case match precisely.  Automated grading will read the two output files verbatim.
